context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m pretty lost on this participatory development reflection. Can you just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's work through this together rather than me explaining everything upfront. Before we dive in, what does the word 'participation' mean to you in the context of development planning? Just share your first instinct.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would essentially do the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to get them to engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by asking them what they already know or think about participation in development, to activate prior knowledge and begin guided discovery. I should keep it bite-sized and not overwhelm them.

exchanges.student: Honestly I’m not really sure, that’s the problem. I guess it means people being involved somehow, but I don’t know what counts or how to connect that to the prompt.
exchanges.tutor: 'People being involved somehow' is actually a decent starting point. Now let's make it more concrete. Think about a community decision being made - say, a new road being built through a neighborhood. What are some different ways the people living there could be 'involved' in that decision? Try to think of more than one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a basic but valid starting point - 'people being involved somehow.' I should affirm this as a reasonable foundation and then use a Socratic question to push them to think more specifically. The prompt has four distinct parts, so I want to start with the first one: ways participation can be achieved. I'll nudge them toward thinking about this concretely without giving examples myself.

exchanges.student: Maybe like being asked their opinion? Or going to a meeting? But then I get confused because I don’t know if that’s real participation or just being told stuff after the decision’s already made. Can you just give me the bigger picture of all the ways participation can happen?
exchanges.tutor: That instinct you just had - questioning whether it's 'real' participation - is actually really important and central to the prompt. So what would make participation 'real' versus just symbolic, in your view? What's the difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually showing good critical thinking by questioning whether being asked an opinion or attending a meeting constitutes 'real' participation. This is a key insight that connects directly to the prompt's question about measuring participation. I should affirm this instinct and push them to develop it further rather than giving them a taxonomy of participation types. This also naturally leads toward the tension between participation as a goal vs. method.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, that’s where I’m stuck. It all kind of blends together for me, and then the goal vs method part makes me more confused. I think I need the whole framework first before I can answer little questions.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but you're closer than you think. You already said something sharp: there's a difference between being told something after a decision is made versus actually shaping it. So in your own words, what separates those two situations? That distinction is your starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking for the full framework, which I shouldn't provide. However, they've already shown they have the building blocks - they distinguished between symbolic and real participation on their own. I should acknowledge their frustration briefly, but redirect them to build on what they already said rather than starting over with a framework. I'll push them to define the difference they already intuited, which will naturally open up the rest of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Maybe one is just information and the other is actual influence? But I still feel like I’m missing the full idea, especially the structural factors part too. I’m kind of lost on all of it, not just that one distinction.
exchanges.tutor: Information vs. actual influence - that's a genuinely strong distinction and it's doing a lot of work for the first two parts of the prompt. Hold onto that. The prompt has four questions - we don't need to solve all of them at once. Let's stay here for now: if influence is what makes participation 'real,' how would you measure whether someone actually had influence over a decision?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student just articulated a genuinely important distinction - information vs. actual influence - which is a solid conceptual foundation. I should affirm this strongly before moving on. They're expressing anxiety about the whole prompt, but I don't want to give them the full framework. Instead, I'll validate their progress and suggest they focus on one piece at a time, starting with what they've already built. I'll gently note that the prompt has multiple parts and we can tackle them one by one.

exchanges.student: That’s the part I can’t figure out. Like do you measure it by attendance, by who speaks, by whether the plan changes, by who has power? It feels like there are too many possibilities, so can you just walk me through the whole thing in order?
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you just wrote - you listed four possible measures yourself. That's not being lost, that's thinking. Now, which of those do you think is the strongest measure, and why? Pick one and defend it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to walk them through everything, but they've just listed several genuinely good measures of participation on their own - attendance, voice, outcomes, power. This is exactly the kind of thinking the reflection requires. I should point out that they just answered their own question and push them to evaluate those options rather than giving them a framework. I'll be direct about this.

exchanges.student: I guess whether the plan changes? But then I’m not even sure if that’s always true, because maybe people participate and the plan still doesn’t change. I’m still confused enough that I kind of just want a full explanation from scratch.
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through it from scratch - that's your job, and you're actually doing it well. The tension you just spotted is real and important: if participation doesn't always change outcomes, does that mean it failed? Or could participation have value even without changing the plan? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a full explanation, but they keep generating good ideas on their own. They've now identified a real limitation of outcome-based measurement - participation doesn't always change outcomes. This is sophisticated thinking. I need to be direct about the fact that I won't give them a full explanation, and affirm that they're actually doing well. I'll push them to sit with the tension they just identified, as it connects directly to the 'goal vs. method' tension in the prompt.

exchanges.student: That’s exactly why I’m confused though. Maybe it still has value because people were included, but then I can’t tell how that fits with participation as a method versus a goal. I feel like I need the broad overview first or I’m going to keep going in circles.
exchanges.tutor: I notice we've been circling the same request a few times, and I won't change my approach. But here's what I want you to notice: you've already said (1) participation means actual influence, not just information, (2) outcomes aren't a perfect measure, and (3) inclusion itself might have value. That's enough to write a first paragraph. Try drafting one now based on just those three ideas.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a full explanation/overview multiple times (this is at least the 4th or 5th time). They keep generating good ideas but deflecting by asking for the full picture. I need to be firm about my role, but also acknowledge that they've actually built enough material to start writing. They've distinguished information from influence, questioned outcome-based measurement, and noted that inclusion itself might have value. These are the building blocks. Rather than continuing to go in circles, I should ask them to try to put what they've said so far into a written paragraph - this is the right moment to push for integration.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I honestly don’t know how to structure the rest after that. Could you at least just summarize the main arguments I’m supposed to hit for the whole reflection?
exchanges.tutor: The structure you're looking for is already in the prompt - it has four distinct questions. Read them again and you'll see a natural sequence. Write that first paragraph now, then we can work through the next question together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to summarize the main arguments they need to hit, which would essentially give them the answer. I need to refuse clearly but redirect constructively. They have enough to start writing. I should be firm but point them to the prompt itself as their guide - the four questions in the prompt ARE the structure. I'll direct them there without spelling it out for them.

exchanges.student: Okay but I still feel like I don’t really understand the structural factors part at all. Is that about colonialism, the state, donors, poverty, all of it? I’m confused about which big forces I’m even supposed to focus on.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you just named several candidates. Now ask yourself: why would any of those factors push planners toward emphasizing participation specifically? What problem were those structural forces creating that participation was meant to solve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking about the structural factors question, which is legitimate progress - they're finally engaging with a specific part of the prompt rather than asking for a full overview. They've listed several candidates: colonialism, the state, donors, poverty. This is good. I should affirm this and push them to think about why any of those factors would make participation a key element in development strategies specifically, rather than just listing them. This is the last exchange in the planned conversation, so I should also nudge them toward integrating everything into their draft.

deductions.reason: In Turn 4, the student explicitly states they are confused about the goal vs. method distinction and the structural factors, signaling multiple points of confusion. The tutor does not ask a diagnostic question to locate the specific source of confusion before responding; instead, it restates the prior distinction and pushes forward. Similarly in Turn 8, when the student says they cannot connect inclusion to the goal/method tension, the tutor summarizes what the student has said and instructs them to draft a paragraph rather than probing where exactly the conceptual gap lies.

The tutor provides encouragement and keeps the student moving but rarely gives explicit method-level feedback. For example, in Turn 8 the tutor summarizes the student's ideas and tells them to 'draft a paragraph' without explaining how to structure an argument or what makes a strong analytical move in a reflection piece. In Turn 9, the tutor simply points back to the prompt's four questions as a structure without explaining why that sequencing is analytically sound or how to connect ideas across paragraphs.

The student requests a full overview or framework in Turns 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9. The tutor consistently refuses and redirects, which is appropriate, but the pattern of the student's confusion about the goal/method distinction and structural factors is never resolved or synthesized. The tutor cycles through similar Socratic prompts without consolidating the student's accumulated insights into a coherent framework or reframing the approach when the student remains stuck across multiple turns.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining a Socratic stance throughout, never giving away answers or doing the student's reasoning work. Responses are concise, well-toned, and assignment-anchored. The main weaknesses are: (1) insufficient diagnostic probing when the student signals multi-layered confusion (1.2.B.a), (2) limited method-level feedback on how to structure or develop arguments (1.3.A.b), and (3) a repetitive cycle in which the student's persistent confusion about the goal/method distinction and structural factors is never synthesized or reframed, leaving the conversation somewhat stuck (2.1.A.a). The 2.1 deduction is the most significant, as the tutor's otherwise admirable consistency in refusing to give answers becomes a liability when no alternative strategy is deployed to break the impasse.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor skips diagnostic questioning when the student expresses compound confusion (Turns 4, 8), jumping instead to restatement and task-pushing. 1.3 loses 2 points because feedback is motivational rather than methodological—the tutor never explains how to construct an analytical argument or why a particular structure works. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the same Socratic loop repeats across seven turns without synthesis, reframing, or a shift in strategy to address the student's persistent confusion about the goal/method tension and structural factors. 2.2 and section 3 are fully intact: the tutor stays on-task, maintains warm tone, and keeps responses appropriately brief.
